beats.narration_text: A model writing a working C compiler over a long horizon fascinates me, because compilers fail loudly on the smallest mistake. I want to understand how the agent stayed on track across so many steps. Help me: (1) list the checkpoints a coding agent could use to verify each compiler stage — lexer, parser, codegen — before moving on; (2) explain how passing test suites acts as reward without a human grader; (3) design a small C program whose miscompilation would expose whether the parser or the code generator is broken.

props.command: A model writing a working C compiler over a long horizon fascinates me, because compilers fail loudly on the smallest mistake. I want to understand how the agent stayed on track across so many steps. Help me: (1) list the checkpoints a coding agent could use to verify each compiler stage — lexer, parser, codegen — before moving on; (2) explain how passing test suites acts as reward without a human grader; (3) design a small C program whose miscompilation would expose whether the parser or the code generator is broken.
output: Claude Opus built a complete C compiler in Rust — no human pair-programming
Lexer, parser, SSA IR, optimizer, code gen, assembler, linker, DWARF debug info
Four target architectures: x86-64, i686, AArch64, RISC-V
Methodology: human writes tests, Claude writes everything else, no help during iteration

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. A C compiler has six stages from source code to binary. Lexer turns characters into tokens. Parser turns tokens into an abstract syntax tree. The AST gets lowered to single static assignment IR. The optimizer rewrites the IR. The code generator emits target assembly. The assembler and linker produce an ELF binary. Every stage is its own implementation. The compiler has no dependency on LLVM, GCC, or any external toolchain.

props.artifactTitle: What a C Compiler Actually Requires
props.artifactHeading: Six stages from source to binary.
artifactLines: Lexer — characters to tokens
Parser — tokens to abstract syntax tree
SSA IR — AST lowered to single static assignment intermediate representation
Optimizer — rewrites the IR
Code Generator → Assembler → Linker → ELF binary (no LLVM, no GCC, no external toolchain)

beats.narration_text: The methodology had one rule. The human — Zack Witten at Anthropic — wrote the test suite. Claude wrote everything else and iterated against the tests. There was no pair-programming, no debugging sessions, no explaining to Claude where it went wrong. The human's only input after writing the tests was: the tests still fail. Claude's job was to make them pass.

props.artifactHeading: Human writes tests. Claude writes everything else.
artifactLines: 1. Zack Witten (Anthropic) wrote the test suite
2. Claude wrote everything else and iterated against the tests
3. No pair-programming — no explaining where Claude went wrong
4. No debugging sessions — Claude's only signal was: tests still fail
5. The constraint IS the methodology — the human's only input was the test suite

beats.narration_text: The compiler targets four architectures: x86-64, i686, AArch64, and RISC-V. It generates ELF executables. A hello world program compiles and runs. This is not a toy tokenizer. It is a complete compiler that turns C source code into machine code that executes on four different processor families.

artifactLines: 1. Target: x86-64
2. Target: i686
3. Target: AArch64
4. Target: RISC-V
5. Produces ELF executables — hello world compiles and runs on all four

beats.narration_text: The README says, verbatim: I do not recommend you use this code — none of it has been validated for correctness. That disclaimer is not a caveat to apologize for. It IS the finding. A model that can build something it cannot be trusted to deploy has demonstrated a capability that matters. The methodology — tests first, no pair-programming, iterate without help — is the exportable insight. The artifact is a proof of concept for the methodology.

artifactLines: 1. The README says verbatim: I do not recommend you use this code
2. None of it has been validated for correctness
3. The disclaimer is not a weakness — it IS the finding
4. Capability and correctness are different questions
5. The exportable insight is the methodology: tests first, no pair-programming, iterate without help

beats.narration_text: Here is the prompt. Write your tests first, then paste this with no other explanation: I am giving you a test suite with no other context. Do not ask me to clarify the requirements. Implement the function that makes these tests pass. When you are done, tell me which tests you are least confident about and why. What the model infers from the shape of the tests is the experiment. Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: Here is a test suite with no other context: [paste your tests]. Do not ask me to clarify the requirements. Implement the function that makes these tests pass. When you are done, tell me which tests you are least confident about and why.
output: implements from tests alone — no requirements document
flags the tests it is least confident about
what it flags tells you what the tests failed to specify
